content.legal_text: Chapter IV: Fundamental Rights
content.plain_english: This chapter outlines the basic human rights and freedoms guaranteed to every person in the country, ensuring their protection under the law.

case_laws.case_title: Olisa Agbakoba v. The Attorney General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: A landmark case affirming the enforceability of fundamental rights in Nigeria and the role of the courts in protecting them.
Cases challenging human rights abuses during military rule, highlighting the importance and supremacy of fundamental rights provisions.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Bill of Rights (Amendments I-X of the US Constitution)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees fundamental freedoms such as speech, religion, and due process, serving as a cornerstone of individual liberties.
Enshrines a comprehensive list of fundamental rights, including the right to life, liberty, and equality, with provisions for their enforcement.
Incorporates the European Convention on Human Rights into UK law, protecting civil and political rights and allowing individuals to enforce them in domestic courts.

content.legal_text: Right to Life
content.plain_english: This section guarantees the fundamental right to life for all individuals, outlining the conditions under which life may be lawfully taken.

case_laws.relevance_summary: A case dealing with the application of the death penalty and the right to life, often cited in discussions on capital punishment.

case_laws.case_title: Gani Fawehinmi v. Inspector General of Police
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases often touch upon the limits of state force and its impact on the right to life, particularly in situations involving public order.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments (Due Process Clause)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees that no person shall be deprived of life without due process of law, requiring fair legal procedures.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 21 (Protection of Life and Personal Liberty)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that 'No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law,' broadly interpreted to include various aspects of life.

content.legal_text: Every person has a right to life, and no one shall be deprived intentionally of his life, save in execution of the sentence of a court in respect of a criminal offence of which he has been found guilty in Nigeria.
content.plain_english: Everyone has the right to live, and the government cannot intentionally take a person's life, except when a court sentences someone to death for a criminal offense they've been found guilty of in Nigeria.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case affirmed the legality of the death penalty as an exception to the right to life, provided due process is followed.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Protects the right to life, with a similar exception for the execution of a sentence of a court following conviction of a crime.

content.legal_text: A person shall not be regarded as having been deprived of his life in contravention of this section, if he dies as a result of the use, to such extent and in such circumstances as are permitted by law, of such force as is reasonably necessary - (a) for the defence of any person from unlawful violence or for the defence of property: (b) in order to effect a lawful arrest or to prevent the escape of a person lawfully detained; or (c) for the purpose of suppressing a riot, insurrection or mutiny.
content.plain_english: A person's death is not considered a violation of their right to life if it results from the use of reasonable force, as allowed by law, for specific purposes: self-defense or property defense, making a lawful arrest or preventing an escape, or stopping a riot, uprising, or rebellion.

case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases involving self-defense or defense of property often refer to the lawful use of force, including lethal force, under specific circumstances.
Discusses the parameters of 'reasonable force' in the context of lawful arrest and prevention of escape.

international_comparisons.country: United Nations Basic Principles on the Use of Force and Firearms by Law Enforcement Officials
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Provides guidelines for law enforcement on the use of force, including lethal force, in self-defense, defense of others, or preventing escape of dangerous criminals, consistent with the 'reasonably necessary' standard.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Dignity of Human Person

content.legal_text: Right to Dignity of Human Person
content.plain_english: This section ensures that every individual is treated with respect and dignity, prohibiting torture, slavery, and forced labor.

case_laws.case_title: Medical and Dental Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal v. Okonkwo
case_laws.relevance_summary: While not directly on torture, cases on professional ethics and human rights often reference the importance of human dignity in all interactions.

case_laws.case_title: Nigerian Bar Association v. Attorney General of the Federation
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases challenging detention conditions or treatment of prisoners often invoke this section to argue against inhuman or degrading treatment.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Prohibits cruel and unusual punishment, which has been interpreted to include torture and other forms of inhuman or degrading treatment.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 23 (Prohibition of Traffic in Human Beings and Forced Labour)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Explicitly prohibits forced labor and human trafficking, aligning with the Nigerian provision against slavery and compulsory labor.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Dignity of Human Person

content.legal_text: Every individual is entitled to respect for the dignity of his person, and accordingly - (a) no person shall be subject to torture or to inhuman or degrading treatment; (b) no person shall he held in slavery or servitude; and (c) no person shall be required to perform forced of compulsory labour.
content.plain_english: Every person has the right to be treated with respect and dignity. This means no one can be subjected to torture, inhuman or degrading treatment, held in slavery or servitude, or forced to perform compulsory labor.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case affirmed the right to dignity and protection against degrading treatment, particularly in the context of traditional practices.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 3 (Prohibition of Torture) and Article 4 (Prohibition of Slavery and Forced Labour)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Directly prohibits torture, inhuman or degrading treatment, slavery, and forced labor, mirroring the protections in the Nigerian Constitution.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Dignity of Human Person

content.legal_text: for the purposes of subsection (1) (c) of this section, "forced or compulsory labour" does not include - (a) any labour required in consequence of the sentence or order of a court: (b) any labour required of members of the armed forces of the Federation or the Nigeria Police Force in pursuance of their duties as such; (c) in the case of persons who have conscientious objections to service in the armed forces of the Federation, any labour required instead of such service; (d) any labour required which is reasonably necessary in the event of any emergency or calamity threatening the life or well-being of the community; or (e) any labour or service that forms part of - (i) normal communal or other civic obligations of the well-being of the community. (ii) such compulsory national service in the armed forces of the Federation as may be prescribed by an Act of the National Assembly, or (iii) such compulsory national service which forms part of the education and training of citizens of Nigeria as may be prescribed by an Act of the National Assembly.
content.plain_english: Forced or compulsory labor does not include work ordered by a court as a sentence, duties performed by members of the armed forces or police, alternative service for conscientious objectors, work needed during emergencies or calamities, or normal communal/civic duties and compulsory national service (military or educational) as defined by law.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Guardian Newspapers Ltd.
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases discussing the limits of state power and individual rights, including what constitutes lawful obligations versus forced labor.

international_comparisons.country: International Labour Organization (ILO) Convention No. 29 (Forced Labour Convention)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Defines forced or compulsory labor and lists similar exceptions, such as work required by military service laws, normal civic obligations, and work required as a consequence of a conviction.

content.legal_text: Right to Personal Liberty
content.plain_english: This section guarantees every person's right to personal freedom and outlines the specific, lawful circumstances under which this liberty may be restricted, along with procedural safeguards.

case_laws.relevance_summary: An early landmark case on unlawful detention and the right to personal liberty in Nigeria, establishing important precedents.
Cases often deal with the 'reasonable time' provision for bringing an arrested person to court, emphasizing prompt judicial review.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Fourth Amendment (Protection against unreasonable searches and seizures) and Fifth Amendment (Due Process)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees personal liberty and due process in arrest and detention, requiring probable cause and fair legal procedures.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 22 (Protection against arrest and detention in certain cases)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Provides safeguards against arbitrary arrest and detention, including the right to be informed of grounds, consult a lawyer, and be produced before a magistrate.

content.legal_text: Every person shall be entitled to his personal liberty and no person shall be deprived of such liberty save in the following cases and in accordance with a procedure permitted by law - (a) in execution of the sentence or order of a court in respect of a criminal offence of which he has been found guilty; (b) by reason of his failure to comply with the order of a court or in order to secure the fulfilment of any obligation imposed upon him by law; (c) for the purpose of bringing him before a court in execution of the order of a court or upon reasonable suspicion of his having committed a criminal offence, or to such extent as may be reasonably necessary to prevent his committing a criminal offence: (d) in the case of a person who has not attained the age of eighteen years for the purpose of his education or welfare; (e) in the case of persons suffering from infectious or contagious disease, persons of unsound mind, persons addicted to drugs or alcohol or vagrants, for the purpose of their care or treatment or the protection of the community; or (f) for the purpose of preventing the unlawful entry of any person into Nigeria or of effecting the expulsion, extradition or other lawful removal from Nigeria of any person or the taking of proceedings relating thereto: Provided that a person who is charged with an offence and who has been detained in lawful custody awaiting trial shall not continue to be kept in such detention for a period longer than the maximum period of imprisonment prescribed for the offence.
content.plain_english: Everyone has the right to personal freedom, and this freedom can only be taken away in specific situations and according to legal procedures. These include: serving a court sentence for a crime, failing to obey a court order, being brought before a court for a suspected crime or to prevent a crime, for the education or welfare of a minor, for the care or protection of the community from individuals with infectious diseases, mental illness, drug/alcohol addiction, or vagrants, or for immigration purposes (preventing unlawful entry, expulsion, extradition). However, someone awaiting trial cannot be detained longer than the maximum sentence for their alleged offense.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case extensively discussed the limits of executive power to detain individuals and the importance of judicial oversight.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Protects personal liberty, outlining lawful grounds for deprivation and procedural safeguards, including detention after conviction, for non-compliance with court orders, or on reasonable suspicion of committing an offense.

content.legal_text: Any person who is arrested or detained shall have the right to remain silent or avoid answering any question until after consultation with a legal practitioner or any other person of his own choice.
content.plain_english: Any person who is arrested or detained has the right to remain silent and does not have to answer questions until they have spoken with a lawyer or another person they choose.

case_laws.relevance_summary: Emphasizes the importance of the right to counsel and the right to remain silent during police interrogation.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Fifth Amendment (Self-Incrimination) and Sixth Amendment (Right to Counsel)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The 'Miranda rights' (right to remain silent, right to an attorney) are a direct application of these constitutional provisions, similar to the Nigerian guarantee.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 20(3) (Protection in respect of conviction for offences) and Article 22(1) (Protection against arrest and detention)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees the right against self-incrimination and the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of one's choice.

content.legal_text: Any person who is arrested or detained shall be informed in writing within twenty-four hours (and in a language that he understands) of the facts and grounds for his arrest or detention.
content.plain_english: Anyone arrested or detained must be informed in writing, within 24 hours and in a language they understand, about the reasons and facts for their arrest or detention.

case_laws.relevance_summary: Emphasizes the constitutional requirement to promptly inform an arrested person of the grounds for their arrest.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that 'Everyone who is arrested shall be informed promptly, in a language which he understands, of the reasons for his arrest and of any charge against him.'

content.legal_text: Any person who is arrested or detained in accordance with subsection (1) (c) of this section shall be brought before a court of law within a reasonable time, and if he is not tried within a period of - (a) two months from the date of his arrest or detention in the case of a person who is in custody or is not entitled to bail; or (b) three months from the date of his arrest or detention in the case of a person who has been released on bail, he shall (without prejudice to any further proceedings that may be brought against him) be released either unconditionally or upon such conditions as are reasonably necessary to ensure that he appears for trial at a later date.
content.plain_english: Anyone arrested or detained for a suspected crime must be brought before a court within a reasonable time. If they are not tried within two months (for those in custody without bail) or three months (for those on bail), they must be released, either unconditionally or with conditions to ensure their appearance for trial later.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case extensively discussed the 'reasonable time' provision and the conditions for granting bail or releasing an accused person.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that 'Anyone arrested or detained on a criminal charge shall be brought promptly before a judge or other officer authorized by law to exercise judicial power and shall be entitled to trial within a reasonable time or to release.'

content.legal_text: In subsection (4) of this section, the expression "a reasonable time" means - (a) in the case of an arrest or detention in any place where there is a court of competent jurisdiction within a radius of forty kilometres, a period of one day; and (b) in any other case, a period of two days or such longer period as in the circumstances may be considered by the court to be reasonable.
content.plain_english: For the purpose of subsection (4), 'a reasonable time' means one day if there's a competent court within 40 kilometers of the arrest or detention location. In all other situations, it means two days, or a longer period if the court deems it reasonable given the circumstances.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case clarified the interpretation of 'reasonable time' in different geographical contexts, emphasizing the practicalities of bringing an accused to court.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Sets out specific time limits for detention without charge, generally 24 hours, with extensions possible under judicial authority, reflecting a similar concern for promptness.

content.legal_text: Any person who is unlawfully arrested or detained shall be entitled to compensation and public apology from the appropriate authority or person; and in this subsection, "the appropriate authority or person" means an authority or person specified by law.
content.plain_english: Anyone who is unlawfully arrested or detained has the right to receive compensation and a public apology from the relevant authority or person, as defined by law.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case affirmed the right to compensation for unlawful detention and emphasized the state's responsibility to uphold fundamental rights.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that 'Anyone who has been the victim of unlawful arrest or detention shall have an enforceable right to compensation,' aligning with the Nigerian provision.

content.legal_text: Nothing in this section shall be construed - (a) in relation to subsection (4) of this section, as applying in the case of a person arrested or detained upon reasonable suspicion of having committed a capital offence; and (b) as invalidating any law by reason only that it authorises the detention for a period not exceeding three months of a member of the armed forces of the federation or a member of the Nigeria Police Force in execution of a sentence imposed by an officer of the armed forces of the Federation or of the Nigeria police force, in respect of an offence punishable by such detention of which he has been found guilty.
content.plain_english: This section's rules about release after two or three months (subsection 4) do not apply to those suspected of capital offenses. Also, this section does not invalidate laws that allow for the detention of armed forces or police members for up to three months if it's part of a sentence imposed by their officers for an offense they were found guilty of.

case_laws.case_title: Gani Fawehinmi v. General Sani Abacha
case_laws.relevance_summary: Cases during military regimes often tested the limits of fundamental rights, including exceptions for national security or military discipline, though often challenged for abuses.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Provides for a separate system of military justice, including powers of arrest and detention for service personnel, which can differ from civilian law, reflecting similar exceptions.

content.legal_text: Right to Fair Hearing
content.plain_english: This section guarantees the right to a fair and impartial hearing for all individuals in both civil and criminal matters, ensuring due process and judicial independence.

case_laws.relevance_summary: Emphasizes the importance of fair hearing in criminal trials as a cornerstone of justice.
A landmark case on the principles of natural justice and fair hearing in administrative proceedings, extending the right beyond just courts.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Sixth Amendment (Right to a speedy and public trial, impartial jury, informed of accusation, confront witnesses, counsel) and Fifth Amendment (Due Process)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Provides comprehensive guarantees for fair trial in criminal and civil matters, ensuring fundamental procedural rights.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article 21 (Protection of life and personal liberty) and Article 22 (Protection against arrest and detention)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: While not a single article, the Indian Supreme Court has interpreted these to include a broad right to fair procedure and natural justice.

content.legal_text: In the determination of his civil rights and obligations, including any question or determination by or against any government or authority, a person shall be entitled to a fair hearing within a reasonable time by a court or other tribunal established by law and constituted in such manner as to secure its independence and impartiality.
content.plain_english: When civil rights and obligations are being decided, including matters involving the government, every person has the right to a fair hearing within a reasonable time. This hearing must be conducted by an independent and impartial court or tribunal established by law.

case_laws.case_title: Adigun v. Attorney General of Oyo State
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case affirmed the right to fair hearing in civil matters, emphasizing the need for an independent and impartial arbiter.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees the right to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial tribunal established by law, for the determination of civil rights and obligations.

content.legal_text: Without prejudice to the foregoing provisions of this section, a law shall not be invalidated by reason only that it confers on any government or authority power to determine questions arising in the administration of a law that affects or may affect the civil rights and obligations of any person if such law - (a) provides for an opportunity for the persons whose rights and obligations may be affected to make representations to the administering authority before that authority makes the decision affecting that person; and (b) contains no provision making the determination of the administering authority final and conclusive.
content.plain_english: A law that allows a government body to make decisions affecting a person's civil rights or obligations is still valid, provided it gives the affected person a chance to present their case to that body before a decision is made, and the decision itself is not final and cannot be challenged.

case_laws.case_title: Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Committee v. Fawehinmi
case_laws.relevance_summary: This case highlighted the importance of providing an opportunity for representation and ensuring that administrative decisions are not final and conclusive, allowing for judicial review.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Requires administrative bodies to act fairly, including giving individuals an opportunity to be heard (audi alteram partem) and avoiding bias, with decisions subject to judicial review.

content.legal_text: The proceedings of a court or the proceedings of any tribunal relating to the matters mentioned in subsection (1) of this section (including the announcement of the decisions of the court or tribunal) shall be held in public.
content.plain_english: All court or tribunal proceedings concerning civil rights and obligations, including the announcement of their decisions, must be open to the public.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case underscored the importance of public trials as a safeguard against injustice and to ensure public confidence in the judiciary.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Sixth Amendment (Right to a public trial)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Explicitly guarantees the right to a public trial in criminal prosecutions, reflecting the principle of transparency in justice.

content.legal_text: Whenever any person is charged with a criminal offence, he shall, unless the charge is withdrawn, be entitled to a fair hearing in public within a reasonable time by a court or tribunal: Provided that - (a) a court or such a tribunal may exclude from its proceedings persons other than the parties thereto or their legal practitioners in the interest of defence, public safety, public order, public morality, the welfare of persons who have not attained the age of eighteen years, the protection of the private lives of the parties or to such extent as it may consider necessary by reason of special circumstances in which publicity would be contrary to the interests of justice; (b) if in any proceedings before a court or such a tribunal, a Minister of the Government of the Federation or a commissioner of the government of a State satisfies the court or tribunal that it would not be in the public interest for any matter to be publicly disclosed, the court or tribunal shall make arrangements for evidence relating to that matter to be heard in private and shall take such other action as may be necessary or expedient to prevent the disclosure of the matter.
content.plain_english: Anyone accused of a criminal offense has the right to a fair and public trial within a reasonable time by a court or tribunal, unless the charge is dropped. However, courts can hold private proceedings, excluding the public (except parties and lawyers), if it's necessary for defense, public safety, public order, public morality, the welfare of minors, protecting privacy, or if publicity would harm justice. Also, if a government minister or commissioner states that public disclosure of a matter is not in the public interest, the court must hear that evidence privately and take steps to prevent its public disclosure.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case discussed the balance between the right to a public trial and the need for private proceedings in certain circumstances, such as national security.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees a public hearing but allows for exceptions in the interest of morals, public order, national security, or where the interests of juveniles or the protection of the private life of the parties so require.

content.legal_text: Every person who is charged with a criminal offence shall be presumed to be innocent until he is proved guilty; Provided that nothing in this section shall invalidate any law by reason only that the law imposes upon any such person the burden of proving particular facts.
content.plain_english: Anyone accused of a criminal offense is considered innocent until proven guilty. However, this doesn't prevent laws from requiring an accused person to prove specific facts.

case_laws.case_title: Woolmington v. Director of Public Prosecutions
case_laws.relevance_summary: A foundational UK case establishing the 'golden thread' of English criminal law that the prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt, reinforcing the presumption of innocence.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments (Due Process Clause)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The presumption of innocence is a fundamental principle of due process in the US legal system, requiring the prosecution to prove every element of a crime beyond a reasonable doubt.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that 'Everyone charged with a criminal offence shall have the right to be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.'

content.legal_text: Every person who is charged with a criminal offence shall be entitled to - (a) be informed promptly in the language that he understands and in detail of the nature of the offence; (b) be given adequate time and facilities for the preparation of his defence; (c) defend himself in person or by legal practitioners of his own choice; (d) examine, in person or by his legal practitioners, the witnesses called by the prosecution before any court or tribunal and obtain the attendance and carry out the examination of witnesses to testify on his behalf before the court or tribunal on the same conditions as those applying to the witnesses called by the prosecution; and (e) have, without payment, the assistance of an interpreter if he cannot understand the language used at the trial of the offence.
content.plain_english: Anyone charged with a criminal offense has the right to: be promptly and clearly informed of the charges in a language they understand; have enough time and resources to prepare their defense; defend themselves or be represented by a lawyer of their choice; question prosecution witnesses and call their own witnesses under the same conditions; and receive free interpreter services if they don't understand the trial language.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case discussed the right to legal representation and adequate facilities for defense, emphasizing the importance of a level playing field in criminal trials.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees the right to be informed of the nature and cause of the accusation, to have compulsory process for obtaining witnesses, and to have the assistance of counsel for defense.

Lists specific minimum rights for anyone charged with a criminal offence, including being informed of charges, having time for defense, legal assistance, examining witnesses, and free interpreter services.

content.legal_text: When any person is tried for any criminal offence, the court or tribunal shall keep a record of the proceedings and the accused person or any persons authorised by him in that behalf shall be entitled to obtain copies of the judgement in the case within seven days of the conclusion of the case.
content.plain_english: When someone is tried for a criminal offense, the court or tribunal must keep a record of the proceedings. The accused person or their authorized representative has the right to get copies of the judgment within seven days after the case concludes.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case highlighted the importance of accurate records of proceedings and the right of an accused to obtain judgment copies for appeal purposes.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees the right to have a conviction and sentence reviewed by a higher tribunal, which implicitly requires access to trial records and judgments.

content.legal_text: No person shall be held to be guilty of a criminal offence on account of any act or omission that did not, at the time it took place, constitute such an offence, and no penalty shall be imposed for any criminal offence heavier than the penalty in force at the time the offence was committed
content.plain_english: No one can be found guilty of a crime for an act or omission that wasn't considered a crime when it happened. Also, the punishment for a crime cannot be harsher than what was legally in effect at the time the crime was committed.

case_laws.relevance_summary: An early Nigerian case affirming the principle against retroactive criminal legislation and harsher penalties.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Article I, Section 9 (Ex Post Facto Clause)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Prohibits Congress from passing ex post facto laws, meaning laws that criminalize acts retroactively or increase penalties for past actions.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that 'No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence, under national or international law, at the time when it was committed. Nor shall a heavier penalty be imposed than the one that was applicable at the time when the criminal offence was committed.'

content.legal_text: No person who shows that he has been tried by any court of competent jurisdiction or tribunal for a criminal offence and either convicted or acquitted shall again be tried for that offence or for a criminal offence having the same ingredients as that offence save upon the order of a superior court.
content.plain_english: No one who has already been tried and either convicted or acquitted by a competent court or tribunal for a criminal offense can be tried again for the same offense or one with identical elements, unless a superior court orders a retrial.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case affirmed the principle against double jeopardy, ensuring that a person is not subjected to multiple trials for the same offense.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Protects individuals from being prosecuted twice for the same offense after an acquittal or conviction.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that 'No one shall be liable to be tried or punished again for an offence for which he has already been finally convicted or acquitted in accordance with the law and penal procedure of each country.'

content.legal_text: No person who shows that he has been pardoned for a criminal offence shall again be tried for that offence.
content.plain_english: If a person has been officially pardoned for a criminal offense, they cannot be tried again for that same offense.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General of the Federation v. Guardian Newspapers Ltd.
case_laws.relevance_summary: While not directly on pardon, cases discussing the finality of legal processes and executive powers often touch upon the effect of pardons.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Grants the President the power to grant pardons for offenses against the United States, which typically prevents further prosecution for the pardoned offense.

content.legal_text: No person who is tried for a criminal offence shall be compelled to give evidence at the trial.
content.plain_english: No one on trial for a criminal offense can be forced to testify or provide evidence against themselves during the trial.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case affirmed the right against self-incrimination, a crucial aspect of fair trial rights.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that no person 'shall be compelled in any criminal case to be a witness against himself,' a direct parallel to the Nigerian provision.

international_comparisons.country: International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Guarantees the right 'not to be compelled to testify against himself or to confess guilt.'

content.legal_text: Subject as otherwise provided by this Constitution, a person shall not be convicted of a criminal offence unless that offence is defined and the penalty therefor is prescribed in a written law, and in this subsection, a written law refers to an Act of the National Assembly or a Law of a State, any subsidiary legislation or instrument under the provisions of a law.
content.plain_english: Unless the Constitution states otherwise, a person can only be found guilty of a criminal offense if that offense and its punishment are clearly defined in a written law. A written law includes Acts of the National Assembly, State Laws, and any related subsidiary legislation or instruments.

case_laws.relevance_summary: This case reinforced the principle of legality, stating that an offense must be clearly defined in a written law for a conviction to stand.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: States that 'No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under national or international law at the time when it was committed.'
States that 'An act may be punished only if it was defined by law as a criminal offense before the act was committed,' embodying the principle of 'nulla poena sine lege' (no punishment without law).

content.legal_text: The privacy of citizens, their homes, correspondence, telephone conversations and telegraphic communications is hereby guaranteed and protected.
content.plain_english: Citizens are guaranteed and protected in their right to privacy, which includes their personal lives, homes, letters, phone calls, and telegrams, ensuring these are safe from unwarranted intrusion.

case_laws.case_title: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
case_laws.relevance_summary: Landmark Indian Supreme Court judgment declaring the right to privacy as a fundamental right under the Indian Constitution.
U.S. Supreme Court case establishing a constitutional right to privacy regarding marital relations.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches and seizures, implying a right to privacy in one's home and effects. The Ninth Amendment suggests unenumerated rights, including privacy.
Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, incorporated into UK law, guarantees the right to respect for private and family life, home and correspondence.
The Indian Supreme Court has interpreted Article 21 (Protection of Life and Personal Liberty) to include a fundamental right to privacy.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Freedom of Thought, Conscience and Religion

content.legal_text: Every person shall be entitled to freedom of thought, conscience and religion, including freedom to change his religion or belief, and freedom (either alone or in community with others, and in public or in private) to manifest and propagate his religion or belief in worship, teaching, practice and observance. No person attending any place of education shall be required to receive religious instruction or to take part in or attend any religious ceremony or observance if such instruction ceremony or observance relates to a religion other than his own, or religion not approved by his parent or guardian. No religious community or denomination shall be prevented from providing religious instruction for pupils of that community or denomination in any place of education maintained wholly by that community or denomination. Nothing in this section shall entitle any person to form, take part in the activity or be a member of a secret society.
content.plain_english: This section guarantees the right to freedom of thought, conscience, and religion for every individual. It includes the freedom to change one's religion or belief, and to express and spread one's religion or belief through worship, teaching, practice, and observance, either alone or with others, publicly or privately. It also addresses religious instruction in schools, prohibiting compulsory participation in religious activities of other faiths for students, and allowing religious communities to provide instruction in their own schools. However, this right does not extend to forming or joining secret societies.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case affirming that the Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment applies to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment, protecting the right to propagate religious beliefs.

case_laws.case_title: S.P. Mittal v. Union of India
case_laws.relevance_summary: Indian Supreme Court case discussing the definition of 'religion' and 'religious denomination' under the Constitution.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: First Amendment (Establishment Clause and Free Exercise Clause)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The First Amendment guarantees freedom of religion, preventing government establishment of religion and protecting individuals' right to practice their religion freely.
Article 9 of the ECHR, incorporated into UK law, protects freedom of thought, conscience, and religion, including the right to manifest and change one's religion or belief.

The Indian Constitution provides extensive rights to freedom of religion, including freedom of conscience, free profession, practice and propagation of religion, and freedom from religious instruction in certain educational institutions.

content.plain_english: Everyone has the right to freedom of thought, personal beliefs, and religion. This includes the freedom to change one's religion or belief, and to express and spread one's religion or belief through worship, teaching, practice, and observance, either alone or with others, publicly or privately.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case establishing the 'Sherbert Test' for evaluating burdens on the free exercise of religion, requiring a compelling state interest.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, Article 10
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Article 10 guarantees everyone the right to freedom of thought, conscience and religion, including the freedom to change religion or belief and to manifest it.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case prohibiting religious instruction in public schools during school hours, citing the Establishment Clause.
U.S. Supreme Court case ruling that mandatory Bible reading and prayer in public schools violated the Establishment Clause.

Parents have the right to withdraw their children from religious instruction and collective worship in UK schools.

content.plain_english: Religious groups are allowed to provide religious instruction to students of their own faith in educational institutions that are fully funded and maintained by that specific religious community.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case upholding the right of parents to send their children to private religious schools, affirming the right to establish and operate such schools.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Article 30 grants minorities the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice, including religious minorities.
The Free Exercise Clause implicitly allows for the establishment and operation of religious schools, as long as they comply with general educational standards.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Freedom of Thought, Conscience and Religion

content.legal_text: Nothing in this section shall entitle any person to form, take part in the activity or be a member of a secret society.
content.plain_english: This section does not grant anyone the right to create, join, or participate in secret societies.

case_laws.relevance_summary: While not directly about secret societies, this U.S. Supreme Court case discusses the limits of freedom of association when it conflicts with compelling state interests, such as anti-discrimination.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Article 9(2) states that associations whose purposes or activities conflict with criminal laws, or that are directed against the constitutional order or the concept of international understanding, are prohibited. This can be interpreted to include secret societies with harmful aims.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Freedom of Expression and the Press

content.legal_text: Every person shall be entitled to freedom of expression, including freedom to hold opinions and to receive and impart ideas and information without interference. Without prejudice to the generality of subsection (1) of this section, every person shall be entitled to own, establish and operate any medium for the dissemination of information, ideas and opinions: Provided that no person, other than the Government of the Federation or of a State or any other person or body authorised by the President on the fulfilment of conditions laid down by an Act of the National Assembly, shall own, establish or operate a television or wireless broadcasting station for, any purpose whatsoever. Nothing in this section shall invalidate any law that is reasonably justifiable in a democratic society - (a) for the purpose of preventing the disclosure. of information received in confidence, maintaining the authority and independence of courts or regulating telephony, wireless broadcasting, television or the exhibition of cinematograph films; or (b) imposing restrictions upon persons holding office under the Government of the Federation or of a State, members of the armed forces of the Federation or members of the Nigeria Police Force or other Government security services or agencies established by law.
content.plain_english: This section guarantees everyone the right to freedom of expression, including the freedom to hold opinions and to receive and share ideas and information without interference. It also grants the right to own and operate media for disseminating information, ideas, and opinions, with the specific exception that only government entities or those authorized by the President under National Assembly conditions can operate television or radio broadcasting stations. This right is not absolute and does not invalidate laws that are reasonably justifiable in a democratic society for purposes such as preventing disclosure of confidential information, maintaining court authority, regulating media (telephony, broadcasting, films), or imposing restrictions on government officials, armed forces, police, or security services.

case_laws.case_title: New York Times Co. v. Sullivan
case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case establishing a high standard for libel claims against public officials, strengthening freedom of the press.

case_laws.case_title: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras
case_laws.relevance_summary: Indian Supreme Court case affirming that freedom of speech and press is a fundamental right, subject only to reasonable restrictions.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The First Amendment protects freedom of speech and of the press, ensuring the right to express opinions and disseminate information.
Article 10 of the ECHR, incorporated into UK law, protects freedom of expression, including the freedom to hold opinions and to receive and impart information and ideas.
Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution guarantees freedom of speech and expression, which includes freedom of the press.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Freedom of Expression and the Press

content.plain_english: Everyone has the right to express themselves freely. This includes the freedom to form opinions and to share and receive ideas and information without being obstructed.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case affirming that freedom of speech may indeed best serve its high purpose when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Section 2(b)
international_comparisons.similarity_note: Section 2(b) guarantees freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression, including freedom of the press and other media of communication.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Freedom of Expression and the Press

However, only the federal or state government, or entities specifically authorized by the President under conditions set by the National Assembly, can own or operate television or radio broadcasting stations.

case_laws.case_title: Red Lion Broadcasting Co. v. FCC
case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case upholding the FCC's fairness doctrine, acknowledging that the scarcity of broadcast spectrum justifies government regulation of broadcasting.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The UK has a regulatory body (Ofcom) that licenses and regulates broadcasting, with a mix of public and private ownership, but with strict conditions for operation.
India has a public service broadcaster (Prasar Bharati) and private broadcasters operate under licenses, reflecting a regulated media landscape.

content.plain_english: This section does not prevent laws that are reasonable in a democratic society from being valid if they aim to: (a) stop the release of confidential information, uphold the power and impartiality of courts, or regulate telephone services, radio, television, or movie screenings.

case_laws.case_title: Attorney General v. Guardian Newspapers Ltd (No 2)
case_laws.relevance_summary: UK House of Lords case concerning the disclosure of confidential information (Spycatcher book) and the public interest defense.

case_laws.case_title: S. Rangarajan v. P. Jagjivan Ram
case_laws.relevance_summary: Indian Supreme Court case discussing film censorship and its balance with freedom of expression, emphasizing the need for reasonable restrictions.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: While robust, U.S. free speech has exceptions for incitement, defamation, obscenity, national security, and contempt of court, which align with these restrictions.

Article 19(2) allows for reasonable restrictions on freedom of speech and expression in the interests of the sovereignty and integrity of India, security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States, public order, decency or morality, contempt of court, defamation or incitement to an offence.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case upholding the constitutionality of military regulations that restrict the speech of service members, recognizing the unique needs of the military.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Hatch Act, Uniform Code of Military Justice
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The Hatch Act restricts political activities of federal employees, and the UCMJ imposes specific limitations on speech for military personnel, reflecting similar principles.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Official Secrets Act, Civil Service Code
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The Official Secrets Act restricts disclosure of information by public servants, and the Civil Service Code outlines conduct, including limitations on political activity and public statements.

hierarchy.section_title: Right to Peaceful Assembly and Association

content.legal_text: Every person shall be entitled to assemble freely and associate with other persons, and in particular he may form or belong to any political party, trade union or any other association for the protection of his interests: Provided that the provisions of this section shall not derogate from the powers conferred by this Constitution on the Independent National Electoral Commission with respect to political parties to which that Commission does not accord recognition.
content.plain_english: Everyone has the right to gather peacefully and to join with others. Specifically, people can form or join political parties, trade unions, or any other group to protect their interests. However, this right does not reduce the power of the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to regulate political parties, especially those it does not recognize.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case protecting the right to associate privately, ruling that Alabama could not compel the NAACP to disclose its membership lists.

case_laws.case_title: Bhim Singh v. Union of India
case_laws.relevance_summary: Indian Supreme Court case discussing the right to form associations and unions as a fundamental right, subject to reasonable restrictions.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: The First Amendment protects the rights to peaceably assemble and to associate with others for expressive purposes.
Article 11 of the ECHR, incorporated into UK law, protects freedom of assembly and association, including the right to form trade unions.
Article 19(1)(b) guarantees the right to assemble peaceably and without arms, and 19(1)(c) guarantees the right to form associations or unions.

content.legal_text: Every citizen of Nigeria is entitled to move freely throughout Nigeria and to reside in any part thereof, and no citizen of Nigeria shall be expelled from Nigeria or refused entry thereby or exit therefrom. Nothing in subsection (1) of this section shall invalidate any law that is reasonably justifiable in a democratic society- (a) imposing restrictions on the residence or movement of any person who has committed or is reasonably suspected to have committed a criminal offence in order to prevent him from leaving Nigeria; or (b) providing for the removal of any person from Nigeria to any other country to:- (i) be tried outside Nigeria for any criminal offence, or (ii) undergo imprisonment outside Nigeria in execution of the sentence of a court of law in respect of a criminal offence of which he has been found guilty: Provided that there is reciprocal agreement between Nigeria and such other country in relation to such matter.
content.plain_english: This section grants every Nigerian citizen the right to move freely and reside anywhere within Nigeria, and prohibits their expulsion from or denial of entry into or exit from the country. However, this right is not absolute and does not invalidate laws that are reasonably justifiable in a democratic society. Such laws may impose restrictions on the movement or residence of individuals suspected or convicted of criminal offenses to prevent them from leaving Nigeria, or allow for their removal to another country for trial or imprisonment, provided there is a reciprocal agreement with that country.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case affirming the constitutional right to interstate travel, striking down state residency requirements for welfare benefits.

case_laws.case_title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
case_laws.relevance_summary: Indian Supreme Court case holding that the right to travel abroad is part of 'personal liberty' under Article 21 and can only be restricted by a law that is fair, just, and reasonable.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Privileges and Immunities Clause (Article IV, Section 2), Fourteenth Amendment
international_comparisons.similarity_note: The U.S. Constitution implicitly protects the right to travel between states and to reside in any state.
Article 19(1)(d) guarantees the right to move freely throughout the territory of India, and 19(1)(e) guarantees the right to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India.

international_comparisons.constitution_provision: Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, Article 21
international_comparisons.similarity_note: EU citizens have the right to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States, subject to limitations and conditions laid down in the Treaties and by measures adopted to give them effect.

case_laws.relevance_summary: U.S. Supreme Court case recognizing the right to travel abroad as a fundamental liberty, though subject to reasonable restrictions.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Article 13 states that everyone has the right to freedom of movement and residence within the borders of each state and the right to leave any country, including his own, and to return to his country.

case_laws.case_title: Satwant Singh Sawhney v. D. Ramarathnam, Assistant Passport Officer, New Delhi
case_laws.relevance_summary: Indian Supreme Court case where the court held that the right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty, but can be restricted by law, such as impounding a passport for criminal proceedings.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: U.S. law allows for restrictions on movement, including passport seizure and travel bans, for individuals charged with or suspected of serious crimes to prevent flight.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: UK law permits conditions on bail, including restrictions on movement or residence, to prevent a suspect from absconding or interfering with justice.

This is only allowed if there's a mutual agreement between Nigeria and the other country for such matters.

content.legal_text: Chapter IV of the Constitution (Fundamental Rights)
content.plain_english: This chapter of the Constitution outlines the fundamental human rights and freedoms guaranteed to all citizens.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Establishes core individual liberties and protections against government overreach.
Details a comprehensive list of fundamental rights enforceable by courts.
Incorporates the European Convention on Human Rights into UK law, protecting fundamental freedoms.

content.legal_text: 42. (1) A citizen of Nigeria of a particular community, ethnic group, place of origin, sex, religion or political opinion shall not, by reason only that he is such a person:- (a) be subjected either expressly by, or in the practical application of, any law in force in Nigeria or any executive or administrative action of the government, to disabilities or restrictions to which citizens of Nigeria of other communities, ethnic groups, places of origin, sex, religions or political opinions are not made subject; or (b) be accorded either expressly by, or in the practical application of, any law in force in Nigeria or any such executive or administrative action, any privilege or advantage that is not accorded to citizens of Nigeria of other communities, ethnic groups, places of origin, sex, religions or political opinions. (2) No citizen of Nigeria shall be subjected to any disability or deprivation merely by reason of the circumstances of his birth. (3) Nothing in subsection (1) of this section shall invalidate any law by reason only that the law imposes restrictions with respect to the appointment of any person to any office under the State or as a member of the armed forces of the Federation or member of the Nigeria Police Forces or to an office in the service of a body, corporate established directly by any law in force in Nigeria.
content.plain_english: This section guarantees that no Nigerian citizen can be discriminated against based on their community, ethnicity, origin, gender, religion, or political views. This means the government cannot create laws or take actions that disadvantage them or give unfair advantages to others based on these characteristics. It also prohibits discrimination based on birth circumstances. However, it allows for laws that set specific requirements for appointments to state offices, the armed forces, or the police.

content.legal_text: A citizen of Nigeria of a particular community, ethnic group, place of origin, sex, religion or political opinion shall not, by reason only that he is such a person:- (a) be subjected either expressly by, or in the practical application of, any law in force in Nigeria or any executive or administrative action of the government, to disabilities or restrictions to which citizens of Nigeria of other communities, ethnic groups, places of origin, sex, religions or political opinions are not made subject; or (b) be accorded either expressly by, or in the practical application of, any law in force in Nigeria or any such executive or administrative action, any privilege or advantage that is not accorded to citizens of Nigeria of other communities, ethnic groups, places of origin, sex, religions or political opinions.
content.plain_english: No Nigerian citizen should face disadvantages or restrictions, nor be given special privileges or advantages, simply because of their community, ethnic group, place of origin, gender, religion, or political beliefs. This applies to both written laws and how government actions are carried out.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Prohibits discrimination on any ground such as sex, race, colour, ethnic or social origin, genetic features, language, religion or belief, political or any other opinion, membership of a national minority, property, birth, disability, age or sexual orientation.

content.plain_english: No citizen shall be granted special privileges or advantages by any law or government action solely based on their community, ethnic group, place of origin, sex, religion, or political opinion, if such privileges are not extended to other citizens.

content.plain_english: No Nigerian citizen can be disadvantaged or deprived of rights simply because of the circumstances of their birth (e.g., being born out of wedlock, or into a particular family).

case_laws.relevance_summary: Nigerian case affirming fundamental rights and challenging arbitrary deprivation.
US Supreme Court case holding that a state cannot discriminate against illegitimate children in wrongful death actions.

international_comparisons.country: Convention on the Rights of the Child

hierarchy.section_title: Right to acquire and own immovable property

content.legal_text: 43. Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, every citizen of Nigeria shall have the right to acquire and own immovable property anywhere in Nigeria.
content.plain_english: Every Nigerian citizen has the right to buy and own land or buildings anywhere within Nigeria, provided they follow other constitutional rules.

case_laws.relevance_summary: Nigerian case affirming the right to property and due process in its deprivation.
Early US Supreme Court case that protected property rights and freedom of contract, though later largely overturned.

international_comparisons.similarity_note: Protects private property from being taken for public use without just compensation.
States that no person shall be deprived of his property save by authority of law (formerly a fundamental right).
Protects the right to peaceful enjoyment of possessions, including property.

content.legal_text: 44. (1) No moveable property or any interest in an immovable property shall be taken possession of compulsorily and no right over or interest in any such property shall be acquired compulsorily in any part of Nigeria except in the manner and for the purposes prescribed by a law that, among other things (a) requires the prompt payment of compensation therefore and (b) gives to any person claiming such compensation a right of access for the determination of his interest in the property and the amount of compensation to a court of law or tribunal or body having jurisdiction in that part of Nigeria. (2) Nothing in subsection (1) of this section shall be construed as affecting any general law. (a) for the imposition or enforcement of any tax, rate or duty; (b) for the imposition of penalties or forfeiture for breach of any law, whether under civil process or after conviction for an offence; (c) relating to leases, tenancies, mortgages, charges, bills of sale or any other rights or obligations arising out of contracts. (d) relating to the vesting and administration of property of persons adjudged or otherwise declared bankrupt or insolvent, of persons of unsound mind or deceased persons, and of corporate or unincorporate bodies in the course of being wound-up; (e) relating to the execution of judgements or orders of court; (f) providing for the taking of possession of property that is in a dangerous state or is injurious to the health of human beings, plants or animals; (g) relating to enemy property: (h) relating to trusts and trustees; (i) relating to limitation of actions; (j) relating to property vested in bodies corporate directly established by any law in force in Nigeria; (k) relating to the temporary taking of possession of property for the purpose of any examination, investigation or enquiry; (l) providing for the carrying out of work on land for the purpose of soil-conservation; or (m) subject to prompt payment of compensation for damage to buildings, economic trees or crops, providing for any authority or person to enter, survey or dig any land, or to lay, install or erect poles, cables, wires, pipes, or other conductors or structures on any land, in order to provide or maintain the supply or distribution of energy, fuel, water, sewage, telecommunication services or other public facilities or public utilities. (3) Notwithstanding the foregoing provisions of this section, the entire property in and control of all minerals, mineral oils and natural gas in under or upon any land in Nigeria or in, under or upon the territorial waters and the Exclusive Economic Zone of Nigeria shall vest in the Government of the Federation and shall be managed in such manner as may be prescribed by the National Assembly.
content.plain_english: This section protects citizens from having their movable or immovable property taken by the government without proper legal process. If property is compulsorily acquired, the law must ensure prompt compensation and allow the owner to challenge the acquisition or compensation amount in court. However, this protection does not apply to general laws concerning taxes, penalties, contracts, bankruptcy, court orders, dangerous property, enemy property, trusts, limitation periods, state-owned corporate property, temporary investigations, soil conservation, or public utility installations (though compensation for damage is required for the latter). Furthermore, all minerals, oil, and natural gas belong to the Federal Government.

case_laws.case_title: Oshodi v. Government of Lagos State
case_laws.relevance_summary: Nigerian case emphasizing the requirement for prompt and adequate compensation in compulsory acquisition.

case_laws.case_title: Kelo v. City of New London

content.legal_text: No moveable property or any interest in an immovable property shall be taken possession of compulsorily and no right over or interest in any such property shall be acquired compulsorily in any part of Nigeria except in the manner and for the purposes prescribed by a law that, among other things (a) requires the prompt payment of compensation therefore and (b) gives to any person claiming such compensation a right of access for the determination of his interest in the property and the amount of compensation to a court of law or tribunal or body having jurisdiction in that part of Nigeria.
content.plain_english: The government cannot forcibly take your movable property or any interest in your land or buildings unless a specific law allows it. content.legal_text: Nothing in subsection (1) of this section shall be construed as affecting any general law. (d) relating to the vesting and administration of property of persons adjudged or otherwise declared bankrupt or insolvent, of persons of unsound mind or deceased persons, and of corporate or unincorporate bodies in the course of being wound-up; (e) relating to the execution of judgements or orders of court; (f) providing for the taking of possession of property that is in a dangerous state or is injurious to the health of human beings, plants or animals; (g) relating to enemy property: (h) relating to trusts and trustees; (i) relating to limitation of actions; (j) relating to property vested in bodies corporate directly established by any law in force in Nigeria; (k) relating to the temporary taking of possession of property for the purpose of any examination, investigation or enquiry; (l) providing for the carrying out of work on land for the purpose of soil-conservation; or (m) subject to prompt payment of compensation for damage to buildings, economic trees or crops, providing for any authority or person to enter, survey or dig any land, or to lay, install or erect poles, cables, wires, pipes, or other conductors or structures on any land, in order to provide or maintain the supply or distribution of energy, fuel, water, sewage, telecommunication services or other public facilities or public utilities.

hierarchy.section_title: Restriction on and derogation from Fundamental Rights

content.legal_text: 45. (1) Nothing in sections 37, 38, 39, 40 and 41 of this Constitution shall invalidate any law that is reasonably justifiable in a democratic society (a) in the interest of defence, public safety, public order, public morality or public health; or (b) for the purpose of protecting the rights and freedom or other persons (2) An act of the National Assembly shall not be invalidated by reason only that it provides for the taking, during periods of emergency, of measures that derogate from the provisions of section 33 or 35 of this Constitution; but no such measures shall be taken in pursuance of any such act during any period of emergency save to the extent that those measures are reasonably justifiable for the purpose of dealing with the situation that exists during that period of emergency: Provided that nothing in this section shall authorise any derogation from the provisions of section 33 of this Constitution, except in respect of death resulting from acts of war or authorise any derogation from the provisions of section 36(8) of this Constitution. (3) In this section, a "period of emergency" means any period during which there is in force a Proclamation of a state of emergency declared by the President in exercise of the powers conferred on him under section 305 of this Constitution.
content.plain_english: This section allows for certain fundamental rights (specifically those in sections 37-41, covering privacy, thought, expression, assembly, and movement) to be limited by laws that are considered reasonable in a democratic society. These limitations can be for reasons like national defense, public safety, order, morality, health, or to protect the rights of others. During a declared state of emergency, the National Assembly can pass laws that temporarily reduce rights related to life (Section 33) or personal liberty (Section 35), but only if these measures are absolutely necessary to handle the emergency. However, even during an emergency, the right to life cannot be suspended unless it's due to acts of war, and the right against retrospective punishment (Section 36(8)) cannot be suspended at all. A 'period of emergency' is defined as a time when the President has officially declared a state of emergency.

content.legal_text: An act of the National Assembly shall not be invalidated by reason only that it provides for the taking, during periods of emergency, of measures that derogate from the provisions of section 33 or 35 of this Constitution; but no such measures shall be taken in pursuance of any such act during any period of emergency save to the extent that those measures are reasonably justifiable for the purpose of dealing with the situation that exists during that period of emergency: Provided that nothing in this section shall authorise any derogation from the provisions of section 33 of this Constitution, except in respect of death resulting from acts of war or authorise any derogation from the provisions of section 36(8) of this Constitution.
content.plain_english: During a declared state of emergency, the National Assembly can pass laws that temporarily suspend or reduce the rights to life (Section 33) or personal liberty (Section 35). However, these measures must be strictly necessary and justifiable to address the emergency situation. Importantly, the right to life cannot be suspended unless it's a consequence of war, and the right against being punished for an act that wasn't a crime when committed (Section 36(8)) can never be suspended.

hierarchy.section_title: Special jurisdiction of High Court and Legal Aid

content.legal_text: 46. (1) Any person who alleges that any of the provisions of this Chapter has been, is being or likely to be contravened in any State in relation to him may apply to a High Court in that State for redress. (2) Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, a High Court shall have original jurisdiction to hear and determine any application made to it in pursuance of this section and may make such orders, issue such writs and give such directions as it may consider appropriate for the purpose of enforcement or securing the enforcing within that State of any right to which the person who makes the application may be entitled under this Chapter. (3) The Chief Justice of Nigeria may make rules with respect to the practice and procedure of a High Court for the purposes of this section. (4) The National Assembly - (a) may confer upon a High Court such powers in addition to those conferred by this section as may appear to the National Assembly to be necessary or desirable for the purpose of enabling the court more effectively to exercise the jurisdiction conferred upon it by this section; and (b) shall make provisions- (i) for the rendering of financial assistance to any indigent citizen of Nigeria where his right under this Chapter has been infringed or with a view to enabling him to engage the services of a legal practitioner to prosecute his claim, and (ii) for ensuring that allegations of infringement of such rights are substantial and the requirement or need for financial or legal aid is real.
content.plain_english: This section establishes how fundamental rights can be enforced. If someone believes their rights under this Chapter have been, are being, or are likely to be violated, they can directly approach a High Court in that state for a remedy. The High Court has the primary authority to hear these cases and can issue any orders, writs, or directions needed to enforce these rights. The Chief Justice of Nigeria can set the rules for how these cases are handled in the High Courts. The National Assembly can give High Courts more powers to enforce these rights and must also provide financial assistance to poor citizens whose rights have been violated, to help them hire a lawyer. This assistance is conditional on the rights violation being substantial and the need for aid being genuine.

content.plain_english: The National Assembly has two key roles: (a) It can grant additional powers to the High Courts to help them more effectively enforce fundamental rights. This aid will only be given if the alleged rights violation is significant and the need for financial or legal help is genuine.
